title: Ibuprofen -- Mechanism of Action
content: The primary mechanism of ibuprofen involves inhibiting prostaglandin precursors. After a physiological or pathological stimulus, membrane phospholipids release arachidonic acid through the actions of the enzyme phospholipase A2. Arachidonic acid is fed into 1 of 3 enzymatic pathways: cyclooxygenase (COX), lipoxygenase (LOX), and cytochrome P450 (CYP450). The cyclooxygenase pathway converts arachidonic acid to prostaglandins, prostacyclins, and thromboxanes. The lipoxygenase pathway yields hydroxy-eicosatetraenoic acids (HETEs), leukotrienes, and lipoxins. Lastly, the cytochrome P450 pathway converts arachidonic acid to HETEs and epoxyeicosatrienoic acids (EET). [24]

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Indications
content: The FDA has approved 2 prescription omega-3 fatty acids products: icosapent ethyl and omega-3-acid ethyl esters. Omega-3-carboxylic acids and omega-3-acid ethyl esters A are prescription omega-3 fatty acids formulations that have since been discontinued. Omega-3-acid ethyl esters, omega-3-carboxylic acids, and omega-3-acid ethyl esters A contain both EPA and DHA, whereas icosapent ethyl contains ethyl esters of EPA only. [3] Iosapent ethyl and omega-3-acid ethyl esters are approved for adults with very high triglycerides (≥500 mg/dL) as an adjunct to diet to decrease triglyceride levels and reduce cardiovascular events. [4] [5] [3] [6]

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Contraindications
content: EPA and DHA can act as alternative substrates for CYP450 metabolism and are partially metabolized by the CYP450 metabolic pathway. However, significant inhibition of CYP450 enzymes by DHA or EPA has not been observed, and no drug-drug interactions have been established with medications that use the CYP450 metabolic pathway. EPA-exclusive supplements have shown to have no drug-drug interactions with other medications that may use the P450 metabolic pathway, such as omeprazole, warfarin, atorvastatin, and rosiglitazone. DHA has been shown to have no interactions with other statin drugs.

title: Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists -- Mechanism of Action
content: The main indication for leukotriene receptor antagonists is in treating chronic asthma. Leukotrienes are synthesized from arachidonic acid by the action of 5-lipoxygenase in many inflammatory cells in the airways. [9] Arachidonic acid is released from cell membrane phospholipids mainly by phospholipase A2. [10] The cyclooxygenase pathway produces thromboxane and prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Corticosteroids inhibit phospholipase A2 and subsequent synthesis of eicosanoid inflammatory mediators, including both prostaglandins and leukotrienes. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs such as aspirin inhibit cyclooxygenases. Zileuton inhibits 5-Lipoxygenase.

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Administration
content: Humans do not possess the enzymes required to synthesize omega-3 fatty acids; therefore, they are considered essential fatty acids because they must be obtained from the diet. Omega-3 fatty acids are consumed in the human diet primarily as fish and plant sources but can also be consumed via prescription omega-3 fatty acid products. [59] Alpha-linoleic acid (ALA) is a common omega-3 fatty acid found in seeds and nuts and can be converted to DHA and EPA inside the body. However, research has found the conversion of DHA from ALA is particularly low, suggesting the importance of direct dietary intake of DHA. [1] [63] Omega-3 fatty acids may be present in several forms, such as triacylglycerols, free fatty acids (FFA), phospholipids, and ethyl esters. [1] Icosapent ethyl, omega-3-acid ethyl esters, and omega-3-acid ethyl esters A are all in the ethyl ester form, whereas omega-3-carboxylic acids are in the free fatty acid form. [6]

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Indications
content: These prescription omega-3 fatty acids products have also been recommended in adjunctive therapy in combination with statins to enhance the reduction of the total cholesterol/high-density lipoprotein cholesterol compared to statin alone. [7] [8] [9] However, some studies have urged physicians to proceed with caution when prescribing a statin/DHA omega-3 fatty acid combination due to the possibility of increased low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol. [7] [10] [11] DHA containing omega-3 fatty acids can be switched to EPA-only icosapent ethyl, which is not associated with increased LDL. [10] [11]

title: Latanoprost -- Mechanism of Action
content: Prostaglandin synthesis occurs in the cell from arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid, a 20-carbon molecule, gets metabolized to leukotrienes (LT) by lipoxygenases or converted to cyclic endoperoxides by cyclooxygenases. The cyclic endoperoxides are metabolized to prostaglandin F2-alpha (PGF2α) by prostaglandin synthase, reductase, and thromboxanes by thromboxane synthase.

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
content: During digestion, the ethyl esters are principally broken down by pancreatic carboxylic acid ester lipase, an enzyme with activity enhanced by high-fat meals. [1] Moreover, the fat content of a meal can affect the absorption of ethyl esters. [1] Subsequently, absorption of the ethyl esters and icosapent ethyl (EPA formulation only) is decreased when fasting, so it is recommended they are consumed with food. [4] EPA is thought not to absorb as well as DHA and is metabolized faster; there is a higher ratio of DHA to EPA within the serum plasma. [58]

title: Lomitapide -- Contraindications
content: Lomitapide can decrease the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Patients with chronic bowel or pancreatic diseases are at higher risk of nutrient deficiencies when using lomitapide. Clinicians should advise patients treated with lomitapide to take daily 400 IU of vitamin E, 80 mg of docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), 110 mg of eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), 200 mg of linoleic acid, and 210 mg of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA). [23]

title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
content: Metabolism: Omega-3 fatty acids undergo metabolism and oxidation in the liver, leading to the synthesis of VLDL to transport fatty acids in the plasma to various tissues. Omega-3 fatty acids-generated lipid signaling molecules are catalyzed by cyclooxygenase and lipooxygenase enzymes. While most DHA and EPA metabolism occurs via beta-oxidation in the liver (as discussed above), cytochrome P450 (CYP)-mediated metabolism is a minor pathway in the breakdown of DHA and EPA. [60]

title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1463
content: Prostaglandiny represent a central class of lipid mediators that function as the parent category for several therapeutic groupings, including drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders and the broader classification HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES. As a structural class, prostaglandiny encompass prostaglandin analogs such as Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] and isopropyl unoprostone, illustrating how specific synthetic agents are derived from the prostaglandin scaffold. In the larger biochemical hierarchy, prostaglandiny are a subclass of ikosanoidy (eicosanoids), linking them to the extensive family of arachidonic‑acid‑derived signaling molecules. This integrated framework underscores the relevance of prostaglandin‑based compounds across diverse medical specialties and highlights their role within both hormonal/vitamin pathways and targeted pharmacotherapy for obstetric, gynaecologic, and urinary conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1426
content: Prostaglandiny are lipid mediators that belong to the broader category of HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES. They are synthesized through the enzymatic action of cyklooxygenasy, which converts arachidonic acid into these bioactive compounds. Prostaglandiny are also members of the ikosanoidy family, indicating that they are a type of eicosanoid. Within the prostaglandiny structural class, unoprostone is recognized as a prostaglandin analogue, and isopropyl unoprostone represents a further structural variant derived from unoprostone. Together, these relationships illustrate that prostaglandiny serve as a parent class for specific prostaglandin derivatives such as unoprostone and its isopropyl form, linking enzymatic synthesis, biochemical classification, and structural analogues in a unified framework.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 271
content: Drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under the prostaglandin family, indicating that these therapeutic agents share the core biochemical characteristics of prostaglandins. The broader category “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES” also lists prostaglandins as a parent group, reflecting the role of prostaglandins as hormone‑like mediators that interact with specific receptors and influence physiological processes. Cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandins, as this enzyme catalyzes the conversion of arachidonic acid into prostaglandin precursors, underscoring its central function in prostaglandin biosynthesis. The term ikosanoidy (eicosanoids) is identified as a parent class of prostaglandins, situating prostaglandins within the larger family of lipid mediators derived from 20‑carbon fatty acids. Isopropyl unoprostone is described as having the structural class of prostaglandins, meaning it is a synthetic analog that mimics the chemical framework of natural prostaglandins and is used for its pharmacologic effects. Conversely, prostaglandins themselves are noted to have the structural class travoprost, indicating that travoprost is a specific prostaglandin analog that shares the characteristic prostaglandin scaffold. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins serve as a central hub linking enzymatic pathways, broader lipid mediator families, therapeutic drug groups, and synthetic analogs used in clinical practice.

title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1100
content: Prostaglandiny represents a core class of prostaglandin compounds that are integral to several physiological and therapeutic contexts. It is a component measured in prostaglandin measurement assays, reflecting its importance in clinical laboratory evaluation. As a member of the broader eicosanoid family, prostaglandiny is linked to cyclooxygenase activity, which mediates its biosynthesis from fatty‑acid precursors. The category of prostaglandiny also serves as a parent classification for drug groups primarily employed in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, underscoring its therapeutic relevance in reproductive and urological medicine. Additionally, hormones, their metabolites, and precursors are organized under prostaglandiny, highlighting its role in endocrine pathways. Within this structural class, specific analogues such as isopropyl unoprostone are identified as having prostaglandiny as their structural basis, while tafluprost is recognized as a prostaglandin‑derived compound that belongs to the prostaglandiny class. Together, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandiny functions as a central biochemical and pharmacological entity linking measurement techniques, biosynthetic enzymes, hormonal metabolism, and targeted drug therapies.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 946
content: Prostaglandiny, representing the family of prostaglandins, are biologically active lipid compounds that are measured in clinical prostaglandin measurement assays as a key component. They belong to the broader eicosanoid class, reflecting their origin from arachidonic acid metabolism, and are closely linked to the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymatic pathway, which governs their synthesis. In therapeutic practice, prostaglandiny serve as the parent class for several drug groups that are primarily employed in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, underscoring their importance in managing conditions such as labor induction, cervical ripening, and bladder dysfunction. Specific pharmaceutical agents—including epoprostenol, isopropyl unoprostone, and the Prostaglandin Analog [EPC]—are classified within the structural class of prostaglandiny, indicating that they are designed to mimic or modify the activity of natural prostaglandins. These analogs are used to treat pulmonary hypertension, glaucoma, and other conditions where modulation of prostaglandin pathways is beneficial. Together, the measurement, biochemical relationships, and therapeutic applications of prostaglandiny illustrate their central role in both physiological regulation and targeted medical interventions.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1415
content: Prostaglandiny functions as a central parent class for a variety of related medical entities. The drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders are classified under prostaglandiny, indicating that these therapeutic agents are derived from or act upon prostaglandin pathways. The category HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES also has prostaglandiny as its parent, reflecting the involvement of prostaglandins in hormonal regulation, vitamin metabolism and receptor signaling. The enzyme cyklooxygenasy (cyclooxygenase) is described as having a relationship with prostaglandiny, underscoring its key role in the biosynthesis of prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. The subclass ikosikanoidy is placed under prostaglandiny, further expanding the family of prostaglandin‑related compounds. The compound isopropyl unoprostone is identified as having the structural class of prostaglandiny, linking its chemical architecture to the prostaglandin scaffold. Finally, prostaglandin A1 is listed as a child of prostaglandiny, exemplifying a specific individual prostaglandin molecule within the broader family. Together, these connections illustrate how prostaglandiny integrates diverse drug groups, biochemical pathways, structural classes and individual prostaglandins, highlighting its pivotal role in pharmacology, physiology and patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
content: Prostaglandins (prostaginy) are biologically active lipid mediators that serve as key components in prostaglandin measurement assays, where their levels are quantified for diagnostic purposes. They are classified within the broader group of hormones, their metabolites, and precursors, underscoring their role as hormone‑like signaling molecules. Prostaglandins also belong to the eicosanoid family (ikosanoidy), representing a specific subclass of these polyunsaturated fatty‑acid derivatives. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandin biosynthesis, establishing a functional relationship between the enzyme and prostaglandin production. Several therapeutic drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary tract disorders are derived from or based on prostaglandins, reflecting their clinical utility in managing uterine contractility, cervical ripening, and smooth‑muscle tone. Isopropyl unoprostone is identified as having a structural class of prostaglandins, exemplifying a synthetic analog that mimics prostaglandin activity. Additionally, prostaglandin endoperoxides (prostaglandinové endoperoxidy) are recognized as downstream products—or “children”—of prostaglandins within the metabolic pathway. Collectively, these interconnections highlight the central role of prostaglandins in physiology, pharmacology, and diagnostic testing.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 251
content: Celecoxib is a chemical agent that targets the cyclo‑oxygenase (COX) enzyme, a gene product that is modulated by drugs and is essential for the biosynthesis of prostaglandins. Cyclo‑oxygenase therefore has a direct biochemical relationship with the prostaglandin family, which together with the broader eicosanoid (ikosanoidy) class constitutes a major pathway of lipid mediators. Within this hierarchy, prostaglandins are classified as a parent group for several therapeutic categories, including drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary‑tract disorders, as well as the broader classification “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES”. Prostaglandin analogues represent a structural subclass of prostaglandins and are employed chiefly as antiglaucoma agents and miotics. The group “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics” comprises bimatoprost, latanoprost, latanoprostene bunod, tafluprost, travoprost and unoprostone, all of which share the prostaglandin structural class. Specific members of this subclass include isopropyl unoprostone, latanoprost, tafluprost, travoprost and unoprostone, each identified as having a prostaglandin structural class. Latanoprostene bunod is further described as a synthetic prostaglandin analogue (inverse ISA relationship). The prostaglandin analog “Prostaglandin Analog [EPC]” is also linked to the same therapeutic group, reinforcing the connection between prostaglandin structural features and their clinical use in lowering intra‑ocular pressure. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how inhibition of cyclo‑oxygenase by agents such as celecoxib can modulate prostaglandin production, while synthetic and natural prostaglandin analogues are harnessed in ophthalmology and other medical fields, reflecting the integrated role of the prostaglandin/eicosanoid pathway in both physiology and pharmacotherapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
content: Prostaglandiny (prostaglandins) represent a major subclass of eicosanoids (ikosanoidy) that are synthesized from arachidonic acid by cyclooxygenase enzymes (cyklooxygenasy). Because of their diverse physiological actions, several therapeutic categories are organized under the prostaglandiny hierarchy, including drug groups primarily used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary tract disorders and the broader classification “HORMONES, VITAMINS AND RECEPTOR SITES.” A number of ophthalmic agents are defined by their structural similarity to prostaglandins. Bimatoprost, latanoprost, travoprost, unoprostone and its derivative isopropyl unoprostone all have prostaglandiny as their structural class, making them prostaglandin analogues. These compounds are grouped together in the category “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics,” which also includes the entry Prostaglandin Analog [EPC]. This therapeutic group is employed primarily to lower intra‑ocular pressure in glaucoma and to produce miotic effects. Thus, prostaglandiny serves both as a parent class for several hormone‑related and obstetric‑gynecologic drug groups and as the structural foundation for a set of prostaglandin‑based antiglaucoma and miotic medications, illustrating the central role of prostaglandin chemistry in multiple areas of patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
content: Antagonisté prostaglandinu function as inhibitory agents of cyklooxygenasy, meaning they suppress the activity of this enzyme that is essential for prostaglandin synthesis. By inhibiting cyklooxygenasy, prostaglandin antagonists reduce the production of prostaglandins, which are mediators involved in inflammation, pain, and fever. This mechanistic relationship underlies the therapeutic use of prostaglandin antagonists in conditions where dampening inflammatory pathways is desired, such as arthritis, acute pain, and other inflammatory disorders. Understanding that antagonisté prostaglandinu exert an inhibitory effect on cyklooxygenasy helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory strategies and anticipate the pharmacologic outcomes of targeting this pathway.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 572
content: Prostaglandins are a subclass of eicosanoids that are synthesized by the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzyme pathway; COX activity therefore has a direct relationship with prostaglandin production. The non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug celecoxib inhibits cyclooxygenase, altering the gene product affected by this chemical and consequently modulating prostaglandin levels. Within pharmacology, several drug groups are organized under prostaglandins as a parent category, including formulations used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary‑tract disorders as well as broader classifications such as “Hormones, Vitamins and Receptor Sites.” Prostaglandin analogues constitute an important therapeutic class, particularly in ophthalmology where they function as antiglaucoma agents and miotics. The group “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics” comprises bimatoprost, latanoprost, latanoprostene bunod, tafluprost, and unoprostone (including its isopropyl derivative). Each of these compounds shares a structural class based on prostaglandins, reflecting their design to mimic natural prostaglandin activity while providing enhanced ocular efficacy. Latanoprostene bunod is specifically identified as a synthetic prostaglandin analogue. Overall, the interconnectedness of prostaglandins with eicosanoid biology, cyclooxygenase enzymology, and a range of clinically relevant drug families underscores their central role in both physiological signaling and targeted therapeutic strategies, especially for the management of glaucoma and related ocular conditions.
